articles.title: IMD seasonal outlook: Odisha among key states likely to face above-normal heatwave days despite mixed temperature trends - Odisha TV
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Odisha
articles.full_text:
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has released its updated seasonal outlook for April–June 2026, indicating a complex weather pattern across the country, with Odisha emerging as one of the key regions likely to witness above-normal heatwave activity, particularly along its coastal belt. While maximum temperatures across much of India are expected to remain normal to below normal, eastern India—including Odisha—along with parts of the northeast and adjoining central and peninsular regions, is likely to experience above-normal daytime temperatures during the hot weather season.
The outlook highlights that coastal Odisha is among the regions expected to record an above-normal number of heatwave days in April, alongside states like West Bengal, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, and Puducherry. This trend is expected to persist through the April–June period, with eastern and central India witnessing heightened heatwave frequency. At the same time, minimum temperatures across the country are projected to remain above normal, indicating warmer nights that could compound heat stress, especially in urban and coastal areas.
For April specifically, the IMD forecasts above-normal rainfall at the national level (over 112% of the Long Period Average), with Odisha likely to receive normal to above-normal rainfall, offering some intermittent relief from rising temperatures. However, this rainfall is expected to be uneven, and the presence of thunderstorms may not significantly offset the heatwave conditions, particularly in coastal districts.
Across India, the outlook points to above-normal heatwave days in parts of east, central, and northwest India, as well as the southeast peninsula. In contrast, some regions of Maharashtra and Telangana may see relatively normal to below-normal minimum temperatures, providing localized relief.

The IMD has cautioned that the anticipated heatwave conditions could have serious implications for public health, water availability, power demand, and essential services. Vulnerable groups—including the elderly, children, and outdoor workers—are at heightened risk of heat-related illnesses. In states like Odisha, where coastal humidity can intensify heat stress, authorities have been advised to ensure preparedness measures such as adequate drinking water supply, operational cooling shelters, and strengthened healthcare response systems.
The weather agency has also urged state governments and district administrations to rely on its early warning systems and impact-based forecasts for proactive planning. The public has been advised to stay hydrated, avoid peak afternoon exposure, and take necessary precautions as India heads into a potentially intense summer season.
